brief_document.title: Implement Gleam conversation handlers and schema derivation
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the Gleam conversation handler behaviour (init, handle_message, handle_timeout, terminate) per ADR-001's conversation-as-fundamental-unit model, and the compile-time schema derivation module that extracts schema definitions from Gleam record types. Conversation handlers let Gleam developers author request-reply and saga logic in an OTP-style callback pattern running in beamr.
brief_document.task: Create conversation.gleam and schema.gleam in sdks/liminal-gleam/src/liminal/. The conversation module defines a behaviour with four callbacks: init (returns initial state), handle_message (processes incoming message, returns updated state and optional reply), handle_timeout (handles inactivity), and terminate (cleanup). The schema module provides compile-time schema derivation from Gleam record types, producing schema metadata compatible with liminal's channel type definitions. IN SCOPE: behaviour definition, callback signatures, schema derivation. OUT OF SCOPE: transport (handled by FFI from SDK-005), channel logic (SDK-005).

requirements.title: Define conversation handler behaviour
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL define a conversation handler behaviour in conversation.gleam that Gleam modules implement to participate in liminal conversations. The behaviour SHALL define a state type parameter that the handler manages across callback invocations.
acceptance: A Gleam module can declare that it implements the conversation handler behaviour
The behaviour is parameterised over a state type S and message types
The behaviour is importable from liminal/conversation

requirements.title: Implement init, handle_message, handle_timeout, and terminate callbacks
requirements.spec: WHEN a conversation is opened, THE SYSTEM SHALL invoke the handler's init callback to produce initial state. WHEN a message arrives, THE SYSTEM SHALL invoke handle_message with the current state and message, returning updated state and an optional reply. WHEN a conversation times out, THE SYSTEM SHALL invoke handle_timeout with the current state. WHEN a conversation is closing, THE SYSTEM SHALL invoke terminate with the final state for cleanup. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT allow a handler to skip any of the four callbacks.
acceptance: handle_message(state, message) returns Result(#(state, Option(reply)), SdkError)
handle_timeout(state) returns Result(TimeoutAction, SdkError) where TimeoutAction is Continue or Close
terminate(state, reason) returns Nil and is guaranteed to be called on conversation end
A module missing any of the four callbacks produces a compile-time error

requirements.title: Implement compile-time schema derivation from Gleam types
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL provide a schema module that derives schema definitions from Gleam record types at compile time. WHEN a Gleam record type is annotated or used as a channel message type, THE SYSTEM SHALL produce schema metadata containing field names, field types, and structural constraints. THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT require external schema definition files for Gleam-native types.
acceptance: schema.gleam exports a derive_schema function or type-class approach for Gleam records
Schema derivation produces field names and their Gleam types as metadata
A record with fields name: String, age: Int produces schema with two fields of corresponding types
The derived schema is compatible with the SchemaMetadata format from the Rust SDK
No external .json or .yaml schema file is required for Gleam-defined types

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement transport or connection logic.
SHALL NOT modify channel publish/subscribe logic from SDK-005.
SHALL NOT re-implement FFI bindings -- uses existing bindings from SDK-005.
SHALL NOT add new dependencies on the Rust SDK beyond what SDK-005 established.
verification: gleam check in sdks/liminal-gleam/ succeeds with conversation and schema modules
gleam format --check in sdks/liminal-gleam/ reports no formatting issues
A test module implementing the conversation handler behaviour compiles successfully
A test module missing one callback produces a compile error
Schema derivation from a test record produces expected field metadata
No file exceeds 500 lines of code

adrs.title: Conversations as the fundamental messaging unit, not messages
adrs.decision: The fundamental unit is the conversation — a supervised beamr process owning a structured exchange between parties — over the message. A conversation mediates lifecycle, failure handling, and (optionally) durability. Exchange patterns (request-reply, streaming, saga) are conversation types with different lifecycle rules, not special-case bolt-ons. The rejected alternative is the NATS/Kafka model: messages as the primitive, patterns assembled from messages.

checklist.text: Conversation handler behaviour (init, handle_message, handle_timeout, terminate) defined as a Gleam behaviour.

stories.text: As a Gleam developer, I want to author conversation handlers as Gleam modules implementing a behaviour so that I can write request-reply and saga logic in a familiar OTP-style pattern.

constraints.text: No file over 500 lines. If a module approaches this limit, extract into submodules.
No panics in production code. .unwrap() and .expect() only in tests.
mod.rs contains ONLY pub mod declarations and pub use re-exports. No logic.
All public types implement Debug. All error types implement std::error::Error.
The Rust SDK must compile with no_std + alloc for WASM targets.
Backpressure responses must never be silently consumed by the SDK. Every pressure signal reaches the application.
Reconnection attempts use exponential backoff with jitter. No fixed-interval retry loops.
The TypeScript SDK must work in both browser (WASM) and server (Node.js) environments without conditional imports in application code.

resolved_context.intention:
When this cluster is complete, developers in three ecosystems can connect to liminal and use its full feature set — channels, conversations, typed messages, backpressure — with APIs that feel native to each language. The Rust SDK is the primary interface for embedded use: Aion workflows, co-located services, and any Rust application that wants zero-hop messaging. The Gleam SDK is the authoring convenience layer: message types defined in Gleam get compile-time enforcement, and conversation handlers are authored as Gleam modules running in beamr. The TypeScript SDK serves browser WASM and server-side Node.js, bringing liminal to web applications and the JavaScript ecosystem.

The experience should be: import the SDK, connect (or embed), and start conversing. Connection management, reconnection, subscription state recovery, and backpressure handling are SDK responsibilities — application code deals with messages and conversations, not transport.
